# Extracao: transcription_mp4_07-Using_Earn-outs_in_Your_Acquisition

- **Fonte:** transcription_mp4_07-Using_Earn-outs_in_Your_Acquisition.txt
- **Tipo:** TXT
- **Tamanho:** 0.01 MB

---

In the last lesson I talked about one form of seller financing. I mentioned that there are actually two kinds, the fixed guaranteed defer payments type and earnouts. So in this lesson we're going to dive deeper into what exactly earnouts are and how they differ from the first kind of seller financing we talked about in the last lesson. Let's get started by covering what an earnout is. With earnouts we are still using the business's future cash flows to make payments. But the money doesn't come from financing or equity. Earnouts are contingent, variable payments to the seller. They aren't guaranteed. Earnouts don't sit on the balance sheet like a seller note. There's no security required. The payments that you make to the seller are performance based in some way. They can be tied to company revenue, profits, cash flow, number of new customers, retaining staff, whatever the parameters are for the earnout. An earnout is useful for situations where you want the transition process to go smoothly. You want the employees to stay on board or clients to be properly taken care of. The seller could stay with the company 30, 60, 90 days to make these things happen. It can be a bonus for the seller to acknowledge some prior work they've done. For example, if the seller sold a large contract that will heavily impact the success of the business, the future success, an earnout could be used as a way to recognize that and secure that seller's interest in ensuring it happens. That creates a win-win situation for both seller and buyer. The seller gets a bonus and you as the buyer get the residual benefits that help the business. It's important when structuring the earnout that you base the payment around incremental free cash flow. This is additional cash flow outside the original structure of the deal. Essentially, this is cash flow that the company would acquire for taking on a new project. If that cash flow is positive, then it indicates that the project is more valuable than not. In other words, you want to use that additional cash flow generated by leveraging the seller to pay for their earnout. You don't want to use the historical cash flow that you've already priced into the deal. This would be incremental. You use earnouts to secure engagement and secure promises, by the way. Let's say you and the seller agree on an earnout structure where you agree to pay them $500,000 and you agree on paying $200,000 of that amount after 90 days if the business hits certain targets. First, they ensure a smooth transition of power from you to them. That means properly transitioning the culture, the employees, the onboarding you into the business, etc. Next, they're introducing you to key partners, making sure the relationships are transferred, making sure that there's continuity in the relationships with suppliers, partners, paying customers, maybe big, large contracts that they secured, but have not received the money for yet. Then finally, they help push through any deals with any new clients or partners so that you can get that money into the business. They ensure the deals are successful. Once those targets are hit, maybe the seller gets part of the earnout, say $200,000, so that first $200,000 is contingent on them helping you do something post-closing. The seller only gets his payout if they hit those targets and those promises are fulfilled. Then the additional $300,000 would get paid out on additional financial criteria over a period of time. Now, I mentioned that earnouts aren't necessarily fixed payments as well as being non-contingent. So the $300,000 could be tied to financial projections as a percentage of payout. If you're projecting revenue growth of $1 million over three years, you could make the remaining earnout a percentage of that growth. Sellers are going to most likely want to tie those earnout payments to targets because they can't control how you run the business. So most likely it'll be built around revenue goals. For example, you offer the seller 6% of revenue over a certain target over the next three years so at the end of the three years, if you hit those numbers, the seller would be paid an additional $300,000. An earnout is most useful after you've reviewed all the financials, created your forecast growth, and for the next 90 days, six months, or multiple years into the future, you use earnouts to get the engagement and the alignment with the seller to ensure whatever they told you when they sold the business comes to fruition so you meet your expectations when you purchase it. When you're doing the forecasting, you're assuming that the business maintains its current contracts or upcoming contracts in progress over a period of time. We cover forecasting in detail in the future model, but it's important to have a general idea of where the business is at, where the business is headed, and ultimately where you want it to be and whatever promises the seller has made to you to ensure that you secure that success. That's a couple ways earnouts can be used in your deal as kind of a security for you and the business. And you can be flexible in how you work out these details with a seller. You can structure the earnout generally any way you negotiate it. One thing for sure I want to mention to you is that you cap earnouts for a seller because if you go in and you make the business explode in value, you don't want to be paying that if it's due to your efforts. So you want to cap the seller's earnouts based on expectations and projections that they've said the business can do based on something they're promising you. And it's a promise to pay if the business succeeds based on the promise the seller made you. Some people enter earnouts where there's no cap at all. This can be very dangerous because if you go through that explosive growth, you can be paying a lot of money to a business owner that did not contribute to that additional success. That's everything you need to know right now about earnouts. Next we're going to be talking about earn ends. This is the ability to use your equity structure to incentivize people to help you build, grow and scale the business to the next level of success. So I'm excited to talk to you about earn ends in the next lesson. I'll see you there.
